advantages: O(1) random access and length lookup.
Amortized O(1) append makes building sequences cheap.
Preserves insertion order, which matters for chat history and ranked results.
Easy to read and slice; the most familiar sequence in Python.
disadvantages: Insert/pop at the front is O(n) — wrong choice for queues.
Membership (`in`) and search are O(n); a set or dict is far faster.
Slicing copies elements, which can be costly for large lists.
Holds Python objects, not packed numeric values — slow and memory-heavy for embeddings (use NumPy/Torch).
